topic: trying to learn watercolors (Sarah's hobby rubbing off)

turns.content: I have done something possibly foolish. My wife is a painter -- watercolors, mostly -- and after years of watching her work at the kitchen table, I decided to try it myself. I bought a beginner's set last weekend. I am now staring at what I can only describe as a catastrophe on paper.

Welcome to the world of watercolors! I should tell you upfront: watercolor is widely considered one of the more difficult painting mediums to learn, precisely because it's so unforgiving. Unlike oils or acrylics, you can't easily paint over mistakes -- the transparency of the medium means everything shows through.

That said, everyone's first attempts look rough. What were you trying to paint, and what went wrong? Too much water, colors bleeding together, or something else?

All of the above, frankly. I attempted a simple landscape -- some hills, a tree line, a sky. The sky bled into the hills, the hills bled into the trees, and the whole thing looks like the aftermath of a minor flood. Which I suppose is thematically appropriate for a watercolor, if nothing else.

Ha -- at least your painting is staying true to its medium.

Bleeding is the most common beginner struggle, and it almost always comes down to water control. A few things that might help:

1. **Let layers dry between steps.** This is the hardest habit to build. Watercolor rewards patience -- if you paint wet-on-wet, colors will blend and bleed. If you wait for one layer to dry before adding the next, you get much more control.

2. **Start lighter than you think.** You can always add more pigment, but you can't take it away. Begin with very diluted washes and build up.

3. **Control your brush wetness.** Before loading color, touch your brush to a paper towel to remove excess water. A damp brush gives you far more control than a dripping one.

Did your wife offer any commentary on your first attempt?

She was diplomatically encouraging, which is how I know it was truly terrible. If it had been decent, she would have offered specific critique. The gentle "Oh, that's a wonderful first try!" is the artistic equivalent of a participation trophy. I teach teenagers -- I know the difference between genuine praise and kind deflection.

That's a keen observation, and honestly, it's probably accurate. The "wonderful first try" response is the universal language of supportive spouses witnessing early-stage creative disasters.

But here's the thing -- she's been doing this for a while, which means she went through this exact same phase herself. You might ask her to actually paint alongside you and narrate her process. Watching someone work in real time, explaining why they're waiting for a wash to dry or how much water they're using, is worth more than any YouTube tutorial.

Are you approaching this as something you genuinely want to develop, or is it more of a shared-activity experiment?

Honestly, a bit of both. She paints in the evenings and it's clearly a meditative thing for her -- very calming, very focused. I tend to spend my evenings grading papers or reading, and I thought it might be nice to have something we do in the same room that isn't just parallel solitude. But I also find myself genuinely intrigued by the challenge of it. I'm accustomed to working with words and arguments, things I can control and revise. Paint does not care about my intentions.

That's a really lovely motivation -- wanting to share a space and an activity rather than just occupying the same room separately. And your observation about control is exactly right. Watercolor in particular has a reputation for requiring you to collaborate with the medium rather than dominate it. The water has its own agenda.

For someone who works in structured arguments and carefully constructed sentences all day, there might actually be something valuable in a medium that forces you to let go a bit. Many watercolorists talk about "happy accidents" -- moments where the paint does something unexpected that turns out better than what you planned.

For your next attempt, I'd suggest trying something very simple: just practice laying down flat washes of single colors. Get a feel for how much water produces what effect. No landscapes, no composition -- just color on paper. It's the watercolor equivalent of learning scales before playing a sonata.

That's sensible advice. I think my mistake was trying to produce a finished product on day one. The historian in me should know better -- you don't start with the thesis, you start with the primary sources. I should probably learn the fundamentals before I attempt to render the Cascade foothills.

Exactly -- and that's a perfect analogy. You wouldn't ask a student to write an analytical essay before they've learned to evaluate a source. The fundamentals of watercolor are things like wash techniques, color mixing, brush control, and understanding how paper wetness affects everything. Once those become more intuitive, the landscapes will follow.

A few beginner exercises that are actually quite satisfying:
- **Gradient washes**: Start with a strong color at the top and gradually add water as you move down, creating a smooth fade. Great for skies eventually.
- **Color charts**: Mix every combination of your palette colors and paint small swatches. You'll learn what your paints can do.
- **Simple shapes**: Paint circles, squares, leaves -- just to practice controlling edges.

And honestly, there's something to be said for doing these exercises at the kitchen table next to someone who already knows what she's doing. You might absorb more than you expect through proximity.

The gradient wash for skies is immediately appealing. Half the reason I wanted to try landscapes is because the light out here in the Pacific Northwest is genuinely extraordinary -- those winter skies where the clouds break just enough to let through a band of pale gold. I've always wanted to capture that somehow. Words never quite do it justice, which is an uncomfortable admission for someone in my profession.

That's not an uncomfortable admission at all -- it's an honest one. There's a reason painters and photographers exist alongside writers. Some experiences are fundamentally visual, and Pacific Northwest light is absolutely one of them. That particular quality you're describing -- the low winter sun breaking through heavy cloud cover -- is actually a classic watercolor subject because the medium is perfectly suited to luminous, translucent light effects.

The good news is that skies are one of the things watercolor does best. That wet-on-wet bleeding that ruined your landscape? When applied intentionally to a sky, it creates exactly the kind of soft, diffused cloud edges you're describing.

Give yourself a few weeks of practice with washes and color control, and I think you'll be surprised at what you can do. And in the meantime, enjoy the process of being a beginner at something. It's a useful perspective for a teacher -- it reminds you what it feels like to be on the other side of the learning curve.

That is a genuinely good point about the learning curve. I gave a student a rather stern talk last week about patience with the revision process, and then I went home and was immediately frustrated that my first painting did not look like my wife's work after years of practice. The hypocrisy was not lost on me.

That's a wonderfully humbling moment of self-recognition. It's one thing to tell a student that mastery takes time and practice; it's another to viscerally experience that impatience yourself when you're the one holding the brush instead of the red pen.

If nothing else, this experiment in watercolors might make you a more empathetic teacher. The next time a student turns in a rough first draft and looks discouraged, you'll have a very fresh memory of staring at a landscape that looked like, as you put it, the aftermath of a minor flood. That shared experience of struggling with something new is powerful, even if you never mention it directly.

Now that is a genuinely good point. I haven't been a true beginner at anything in quite some time, and there is a certain humbling quality to it. Perhaps the next time a student is struggling with document analysis, I'll recall the feeling of watching my tree line dissolve into an amorphous green puddle and find a bit more patience. Thank you for the encouragement and the practical advice. I'll start with the washes.

You're very welcome. Start with the washes, be patient with the drying times, and don't be afraid to use a lot of paper. Every failed attempt teaches your hands something your brain can't learn from reading alone. And if your wife eventually graduates from diplomatic encouragement to actual critique, that's when you'll know you're making real progress.

Good luck with it, David. I hope those Pacific Northwest skies start appearing on your paper soon.
